seoTitle: Linco Technical Support | Motor Control Resources | LiTong Distributor
seoDescription: Access Linco technical support, application notes, and design guides. FAE assistance for motor control MCU selection and algorithm development.

faqs.question: What technical support does LiTong provide for Linco products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical support for Linco products including: (1) MCU selection guidance - helping choose the right LKS series for your motor control application; (2) Hardware design review - schematic and PCB layout review for motor drive designs; (3) Software support - FOC algorithm tuning, debugging assistance, code optimization; (4) Reference designs - access to proven designs for home appliances, automotive, and industrial applications; (5) Development tools - evaluation boards and debugging tools available; (6) Training - technical seminars on motor control and Linco MCU usage. Our FAE team has extensive motor control experience and can help accelerate your product development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team through the support portal or schedule a design review meeting.

faqs.question: How do I get started with Linco product evaluation?
faqs.answer: Getting started with Linco products: (1) Sample request - contact LiTong sales for free MCU samples; (2) Development boards - order evaluation kits for your target LKS series; (3) Software tools - download Keil MDK with Linco device packs; (4) Documentation - datasheets, user manuals, and application notes available; (5) Reference code - motor control libraries and example projects; (6) FAE support - technical guidance for your specific application. LiTong maintains stock of popular Linco MCUs for quick sample delivery and provides local technical support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with samples and development boards, then engage FAE for application-specific guidance.

faqs.question: What documentation is available for Linco products?
faqs.answer: Available Linco documentation includes: (1) Datasheets - electrical specifications, pin configurations, timing diagrams; (2) User Manuals - detailed register descriptions, peripheral operation; (3) Application Notes - motor control algorithms, hardware design guides, tuning procedures; (4) Reference Designs - complete schematics, BOMs, and PCB layouts; (5) Software Libraries - motor control firmware with documentation; (6) Quick Start Guides - getting started with development tools; (7) Safety Manuals - functional safety documentation for automotive applications. All documentation is available through LiTong's support portal in both English and Chinese.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request the complete documentation package for your target MCU series.

articles.title: Linco Motor Control MCU Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting Linco motor control MCUs based on motor type, control algorithm, performance requirements, and application environment.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: The most common question I get is 'Which Linco MCU should I use?' The answer depends on your control algorithm. For simple trapezoidal BLDC, the LKS03 is perfect - it's cost-effective and has all the features you need. For FOC applications, you need the LKS05 or LKS07 with the dedicated DSP. The DSP makes a huge difference - it can calculate sin/cos in 100ns versus 5-10 microseconds on a generic ARM core. That's the difference between a 10kHz control loop and a 20kHz control loop. For automotive, the LKS32AT series is the obvious choice with AEC-Q100 qualification. One tip: if you're unsure, start with the LKS05 - it hits the sweet spot of performance and cost for most applications.
faeInsights.logic: MCU selection hierarchy: (1) Determine motor type and control algorithm; (2) Calculate required processing performance; (3) Decide on integration level (external vs integrated driver); (4) Consider operating environment (consumer vs automotive); (5) Evaluate memory requirements; (6) Check analog peripheral needs; (7) Factor in cost constraints.
keyTakeaways: Use LKS03 for simple BLDC, LKS05/LKS07 for FOC
Dedicated DSP essential for complex algorithms
Consider integration level for BOM optimization
bestPractices: Start with reference design closest to your application
Evaluate algorithm complexity before MCU selection
Prototype with development board before custom design
troubleshootingTips: Verify hardware connections before debugging software
Check power supply voltages and stability
Use oscilloscope to verify signal integrity
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Linco Motor Control MCU Selection Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

faqs.question: How do I determine if I need the dedicated DSP?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DSP for FOC, high-speed, or complex algorithms. Simple BLDC can use ARM core only.

faqs.question: What memory size do I need for motor control applications?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate library + application + margin. Choose next size up for future expansion.

articles.title: FOC Implementation with Linco MCUs
articles.summary: Step-by-step guide to implementing Field Oriented Control using Linco LKS05/LKS07 MCUs with dedicated DSP.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: FOC implementation is where the Linco DSP really shines. I've helped dozens of customers implement FOC, and the key is using the DSP for the math-intensive parts. The DSP can do sin/cos in 100ns - that's game-changing for FOC. Here's my recommended approach: (1) Use the Linco FOC library as a starting point - it's well-optimized and tested; (2) Tune the PI controllers for your specific motor - start with Kp=1, Ki=0.1 and adjust from there; (3) For sensorless, spend time tuning the observer - it's the heart of sensorless FOC; (4) Current sampling is critical - use dual-sampling ADC and trigger at the right point in the PWM cycle. One common mistake is trying to run FOC on a generic ARM core without DSP - it works but you'll be limited to slow control loops. With the Linco DSP, you can easily achieve 10-20kHz current loops.
faeInsights.logic: FOC implementation steps: (1) Select MCU with DSP (LKS05/LKS07); (2) Design hardware with proper current sensing; (3) Start with Linco FOC library; (4) Configure motor parameters; (5) Tune current PI controllers; (6) Tune speed PI controller; (7) Implement sensorless observer if needed; (8) Add MTPA and field weakening; (9) Test across full speed and load range.
keyTakeaways: Use Linco FOC library as starting point
Tune PI controllers for your specific motor
commonPitfalls: Running FOC without DSP - too slow
Observer not matched to motor parameters
bestPractices: Use dual-sampling ADC for simultaneous phase current
Trigger ADC at optimal point in PWM cycle
Start with conservative PI gains and increase gradually
Validate observer performance at low speeds
troubleshootingTips: Verify hardware connections before debugging software
Check power supply voltages and stability
Use oscilloscope to verify signal integrity
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this FOC Implementation with Linco MCUs article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sensored and sensorless FOC?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sensored for zero-speed torque or highest precision. Use sensorless for cost-sensitive or reliability-critical applications.

faqs.question: How do I tune the PI controllers for FOC?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Tune current loop first, then speed loop. Start conservative and increase gradually. Use auto-tuning if available.

articles.title: Hardware Design Guide for Motor Control
articles.summary: Best practices for hardware design including current sensing, gate drive, PCB layout, and protection circuits for Linco motor control applications.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: Hardware design can make or break a motor control system. I've seen great software fail because of poor hardware design. The #1 rule is minimize the switching loop area - the loop formed by the MOSFETs, DC link capacitor, and gate driver. Keep this loop as small as possible to reduce EMI and voltage spikes. For current sensing, I strongly recommend Kelvin connections - separate traces for current path and sense points. This eliminates errors from PCB trace resistance. For gate drive, don't skimp on gate resistors - they prevent ringing and EMI. Start with 10Ω and adjust based on waveforms. One often overlooked aspect is the DC link capacitor - use ceramic capacitors close to the MOSFETs, not just electrolytic bulk capacitors. The ceramics handle the high-frequency ripple current.
faeInsights.logic: Hardware design framework: (1) Select current sensing topology based on accuracy and cost requirements; (2) Size gate drive for MOSFET gate charge; (3) Design PCB with minimal switching loops; (4) Implement proper grounding strategy; (5) Add comprehensive protection circuits; (6) Design for thermal management; (7) Minimize EMI through layout.
keyTakeaways: Use Kelvin connections for current sensing
Add gate resistors to prevent ringing
commonPitfalls: Large switching loop area causing EMI
bestPractices: Place ceramic capacitors directly at MOSFET bridge
Use 4-wire Kelvin connection for shunt resistors
Start with 10Ω gate resistor and optimize
Separate power ground and signal ground at single point
troubleshootingTips: Verify hardware connections before debugging software
Check power supply voltages and stability
Use oscilloscope to verify signal integrity
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Hardware Design Guide for Motor Control article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

faqs.question: What is the best current sensing topology for my application?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use single shunt for cost-sensitive BLDC. Use dual/triple shunt for FOC. Use isolated sensors for high voltage or isolation requirements.

faqs.question: How do I size the DC link capacitor?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on ripple requirements. Use electrolytic + ceramic combination. Place ceramics at MOSFETs.

articles.title: Motor Control Troubleshooting Guide
articles.summary: Common motor control issues and diagnostic procedures including start-up failures, instability, noise, and protection trips.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: Troubleshooting motor control systems requires systematic diagnosis. When a motor won't start, I always check these in order: (1) Hardware - verify gate drive signals with scope, check DC bus voltage, confirm MOSFETs switching; (2) Current sensing - verify ADC readings match actual current; (3) Position feedback - for sensored, check encoder signals; for sensorless, check observer output; (4) Software - verify PWM outputs, check control loop execution. For instability, 90% of the time it's PI controller tuning - reduce gains and see if stability improves. For protection trips, check if they're real faults or nuisance trips - adjust thresholds if needed. One invaluable tool is a current probe - being able to see phase currents tells you so much about what's happening. Always start with the basics before diving into complex algorithm issues.
faeInsights.logic: Troubleshooting approach: (1) Verify hardware operation first - gate drive, power stage; (2) Check current sensing accuracy; (3) Validate position feedback; (4) Review software configuration; (5) Analyze control loop behavior; (6) Check protection settings; (7) Test under controlled conditions; (8) Document findings and solutions.
keyTakeaways: Use current probe to observe phase currents
Most instability is PI tuning related
Distinguish real faults from nuisance trips
commonPitfalls: Debugging software when hardware has issues
bestPractices: Use scope to verify gate drive signals
Check current sensing with known load
troubleshootingTips: Verify hardware connections before debugging software
Check power supply voltages and stability
Use oscilloscope to verify signal integrity
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Motor Control Troubleshooting Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

faqs.question: Why does my sensorless motor fail to start?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement proper open-loop start sequence. Use align-and-go. Gradual acceleration. Check current limits.

faqs.question: What causes motor current ripple and how do I reduce it?
faqs.answer: Motor current ripple causes and solutions: (1) PWM ripple - inherent to switching, reduce by increasing PWM frequency or adding inductance; (2) Dead-time distortion - causes current distortion at zero-crossing, minimize dead-time or compensate in software; (3) Current sampling timing - sampling at wrong point in PWM cycle causes aliasing, synchronize ADC with PWM; (4) Sensor noise - inadequate filtering on current sensors, add RC filter; (5) Control instability - oscillating current loop, reduce PI gains. Measurement: use current probe to see actual ripple vs control reference. Solutions: (1) Increase PWM frequency to 15-20kHz; (2) Optimize dead-time; (3) Synchronize sampling; (4) Add output inductor if needed; (5) Tune current controller. Some ripple is normal - typically 10-20% of rated current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check sampling timing first. Increase PWM frequency. Optimize dead-time. Tune current loop.
